CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (end-to-end global optimization, syntactic feature integration, LSTM-Minus segments) with experiments on ACE05 and CONLL04 and reported hyperparameters. Notation and writing are generally clear and grammatical, figures/tables appear informative and related work coverage is thorough. Presentation could be improved by clearer, more explicit disentangling of the many architectural components (per-component ablations and clearer exposition of the beam-size/decoding tradeoff are only briefly treated), hence not perfect but overall well written. | B(4): The paper is well-organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states main contributions early, including the global optimization approach and use of parser-derived syntactic features. Notation and model components are generally consistent and well-defined, and the experiments/tables effectively present results on ACE05 and CONLL04 with clear hyperparameter reporting. Writing quality is good and related work coverage is thorough; minor presentation issues remain (e.g., practical decoding-speed tradeoffs could be emphasized more and code availability is not mentioned).

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (end-to-end global optimization, syntactic feature integration, LSTM-Minus segments) with experiments on ACE05 and CONLL04 and reported hyperparameters. Notation and writing are generally clear and grammatical, figures/tables appear informative and related work coverage is thorough. Presentation could be improved by clearer, more explicit disentangling of the many architectural components (per-component ablations and clearer exposition of the beam-size/decoding tradeoff are only briefly treated), hence not perfect but overall well written.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well-organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states main contributions early, including the global optimization approach and use of parser-derived syntactic features. Notation and model components are generally consistent and well-defined, and the experiments/tables effectively present results on ACE05 and CONLL04 with clear hyperparameter reporting. Writing quality is good and related work coverage is thorough; minor presentation issues remain (e.g., practical decoding-speed tradeoffs could be emphasized more and code availability is not mentioned).

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper is a strong fit for a top NLP/ML venue, delivering substantial empirical gains on standard benchmarks and addressing an important end-to-end relation extraction problem. Reviewer A’s concerns about formal analysis of the “global” training claims and the reliance on an external parser are valid but constitute minor methodological caveats rather than misalignment with the venue; given the solid empirical results and clear community relevance, a top-tier fit is justified.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper sits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML scope: end-to-end neural relation extraction with global training and evaluation on ACE05 and CONLL04 is of clear interest to this community. However, from my theoretical purist perspective there are minor fit concerns: the claim of a “global” optimization procedure (beam-search with early update) lacks formal analysis of its properties (convergence/margin/consistency), and the heavy reliance on a strong external dependency parser (Dozat & Manning 2016) is not clearly quantified. These issues do not disqualify the work for a top venue but warrant stricter expectations for ablations and theoretical justification.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it addresses end-to-end relation extraction with a strong empirical focus, reporting sizable, reproducible SOTA gains on standard benchmarks (ACE05 +1.9%, CONLL04 +6.8%) and comparing to solid baselines (Miwa & Bansal 2016, Li & Ji 2014, etc.). The formulation (table-filling joint model), use of expected methods (BiLSTMs, char-CNNs, syntactic encoder features, LSTM-Minus segments) and evaluation (micro F1 on ACE05/CONLL04) meet community standards, and the global beam-search optimization plus ablation-style claims about syntactic and segment features have direct practical relevance for production systems. Minor engineering limitations (beam-size decoding speed) do not undermine topical fit or audience relevance.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper provides solid empirical validation on standard benchmarks with appropriate baselines, reports hyperparameters, and examines useful components (syntactic encodings, LSTM-Minus), while noting decoding-speed/beam-size limitations and no code release. The main shortcoming is the lack of rigorous statistical significance/variance reporting and deeper analysis of the parser contribution and training theory; these reduce reproducibility and understanding but do not negate the clear empirical gains, so a score of 4 is warranted.

reviewer_a.justification: The experimental suite covers two standard benchmarks (ACE05, CONLL04) and compares to appropriate baselines, and the authors report hyperparameters and some component-level claims (syntactic features, LSTM-Minus segments). However, from the summary there is no rigorous ablation isolating each architectural component or quantification of the parser’s contribution, no statistical significance or variance estimates for the reported gains, and no theoretical analysis of the proposed global training procedure (convergence, consistency) — all critical for the paper’s central claims. The paper does acknowledge a decoding-speed limitation with beam size, but code availability is unmentioned, reducing reproducibility despite reported hyperparameters.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents solid empirical validation across two standard benchmarks (ACE05, CONLL04) and compares to strong baselines (Miwa & Bansal 2016, Li & Ji 2014, etc.), claiming clear SOTA gains. It also includes sensible ablations/sensitivity claims (showing benefits from parser-derived syntactic encodings and LSTM-Minus segment features) and contrasts global beam-search training with local cross-entropy training, though beam-size decoding speed is noted as a practical limitation. Related work appears thoroughly covered and hyperparameters are reported, but code is not mentioned and decoding speed constraints/beam-size sensitivity could use deeper analysis for full reproducibility and production deployment guidance.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper assembles known components into a coherent end-to-end relation extraction system and that the main distinction is the global optimization/beam-search training rather than a wholly new paradigm. Given the substantial empirical gains and the methodological integration of global training into a table-filling framework—which meaningfully advances prior local approaches—I rate novelty as notable but not groundbreaking, hence a score of 4.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper combines several existing components (table-filling formulation, bi-LSTMs, character CNNs, parser-derived features, and LSTM-Minus segments) and applies a beam-search global optimization (early-update) to end-to-end relation extraction; this is a coherent engineering advance but not a fundamentally new paradigm. While the integration and empirical gains on ACE05 and CONLL04 are notable, the approach reads as a non-trivial yet incremental extension of prior joint neural models (e.g., Miwa & Bansal 2016) rather than a radically novel method. From a theoretical perspective the global training claim lacks formal analysis or guarantees and the contribution of individual components appears insufficiently isolated, so the novelty is moderate rather than high.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper combines several known components (bi-LSTMs, char CNNs, parser-derived syntactic encodings and LSTM-Minus segment features) but applies them within a globally optimized, table-filling end-to-end framework with beam-search early-update training — a clear methodological integration that is not merely a rehash of prior local training approaches. The explicit focus on global optimization for joint entity and relation extraction and the reported substantial SOTA gains (notably +1.9% on ACE05 and +6.8% on CONLL04) differentiate it from prior work like Miwa & Bansal (2016) and earlier statistical models. While the individual techniques are largely known, their non-trivial combination and demonstrated empirical benefit would inform experts and constitute notable novelty rather than a purely incremental contribution.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important end-to-end relation extraction problem and demonstrates notable, reproducible empirical gains. While Reviewer A rightly notes limited theoretical analysis and unclear isolation of component contributions, Reviewer B’s point that the work provides a clear, practical engineering recipe with global training that others can adopt supports significant practical impact. Given the strong empirical improvements and likely influence on future joint modeling work despite limited theoretical novelty, I assign a score of 4.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important, practical problem (end-to-end relation extraction) and reports nontrivial state-of-the-art gains (≈1.9% on ACE05, 6.8% on CONLL04), so it will attract attention from applied practitioners. However, from a theoretical/impact perspective the contribution is limited: the claimed “global optimization” lacks theoretical analysis (convergence, consistency or margin guarantees) and the model glues together many strong components (parser-derived syntactic encoders, LSTM-Minus segments, character CNNs) without clear isolation of each contribution or quantification of reliance on external resources. Those issues reduce the likelihood that this will fundamentally change how researchers think about end-to-end extraction, though it is a useful empirical step that others will build on.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses an important, practical problem—end-to-end relation extraction—and demonstrates substantial, reproducible empirical gains (±1.9% on ACE05 and a large 6.8% on CONLL04) using a globally optimized joint model. The combination of beam-search global optimization, incorporation of strong dependency-parser features, and segment (LSTM-Minus) representations provides a clear engineering recipe that practitioners and researchers can adopt and extend. While the advance is unlikely to be purely transformative across all NLP, it is a strong, practically impactful contribution that will likely steer future work on joint modeling and global training for information extraction.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper presents promising empirical gains on standard benchmarks and uses sensible modeling choices, but they also raise the same practical concerns: absence of theoretical guarantees for the global beam-search optimization, limited ablation/statistical significance analysis, and reproducibility constraints (beam cap and no code). Given these shortcomings materially weaken confidence in the claimed contributions despite solid empirical results, a conservative score of 3 is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides empirical evidence of improved end-to-end performance on ACE05 and CONLL04 against appropriate baselines, and reports hyperparameters and realistic decoding constraints, which supports the core experimental claim. However, from the summary I see no theoretical analysis of the proposed ‘‘global’’ beam-search optimization (no convergence/margin/consistency guarantees) and the work appears to conflate multiple architectural additions (parser features, LSTM-Minus, label LSTM) without clear, rigorous ablation or variance/statistical significance testing quantifying each contribution. The decoding-speed constraint (beam size capped at 5) and lack of code also limit reproducibility and raise concerns about robustness. Overall the empirical results are promising but important theoretical and experimental controls are missing, so I cannot rate it higher.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents clear claims and provides strong empirical evidence on standard benchmarks (ACE05, CONLL04) with reported SOTA gains (≈1.9% and 6.8%) and compares to appropriate neural and statistical baselines, which fits my preference for substantial empirical validation. The modeling and training choices (table-filling formulation, global beam-search optimization, integration of parser-derived features and segment encodings) are technically plausible and evaluated against sensible baselines; there are no apparent mathematical errors in the described approach. Minor concerns remain: no mention of statistical significance testing, limited discussion of decoding/beam-size tradeoffs beyond a practical cap at 5, and code/reproducibility details are not provided — these do not invalidate the results but prevent a fully flawless assessment. Overall the technical claims are well supported but would benefit from significance tests and release of code for reproducibility.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper compares to appropriate, recent baselines on standard ACE05 and CONLL04 splits using the same micro-F1 metric and reports ablations and variants (local vs global), yielding strong SOTA gains. However, Reviewer A rightly flags missing statistical-significance testing and incomplete analysis of reliance on external parser features, so while comparisons are largely fair and thorough, the lack of deeper robustness checks prevents a top score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares to appropriate and recent baselines (Miwa & Bansal 2016; Miwa & Sasaki 2014; Li & Ji 2014; Gupta et al. 2016) on the standard ACE05 and CONLL04 splits using micro F1, so head-to-head comparisons are largely fair. However, from the summary it is unclear whether differences are accompanied by statistical significance or rigorous ablation isolating each contribution (notably the strong dependency parser features and segment/LSTM-Minus components), and the reliance on an external high-quality parser is not quantified—so while comparisons are good, they lack the deeper analysis and robustness checks I expect.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates on standard benchmarks (ACE05, CONLL04) using the same micro F1 metric and compares to strong, recent baselines (Miwa & Bansal 2016, Gupta et al. 2016, Miwa & Sasaki 2014, Li & Ji 2014). It contrasts locally trained vs globally optimized variants (beam-search/early-update) and reports ablations (syntactic encoder, segment representations), acknowledges limitations (beam-size speed), and claims clear SOTA gains, indicating comprehensive and fair comparisons with relevant competing approaches.

main_claims: Introducing a globally optimized end-to-end neural model for joint entity detection and relation extraction improves performance over locally trained neural and prior statistical models.
Integrating syntactic features derived from a strong dependency parser (Dozat & Manning 2016) into word representations significantly improves relation extraction performance.
Using segment representations (LSTM-Minus) and explicit modeling of entity-segment features benefits both entity detection and relation classification.
Their final model achieves state-of-the-art results on two benchmarks (ACE05 and CONLL04), improving prior best reported results by 1.9% (ACE05) and 6.8% (CONLL04).
_summary.methodology: Cast end-to-end relation extraction as a table-filling problem and use neural representations from three LSTMs (bi-directional word LSTMs and a label LSTM), augmented with character CNNs, parser-derived syntactic encoder hidden layers, and LSTM-Minus segment features; train with beam-search global optimization (early-update) and compare to locally trained (cross-entropy) models.
limitations: Decoding speed becomes intolerably slow when the beam size increases beyond 5 (they therefore use beam size 5).
Local (stepwise) training can suffer from label bias, which motivates the global optimization approach.

reviewer_a.name: Theory Purist
reviewer_a.background: Associate professor in computational linguistics with a focus on learning theory and structured prediction. Has published work on provable guarantees for structured models and global optimization algorithms.
traits: Requires formal/theoretical justification for global training objectives or optimization procedures; will deduct points if a proposed 'global' method lacks analysis of properties (e.g., convergence, margin, or consistency) or clear motivation beyond empirical gains.

Penalises papers that combine many architectural components without rigorous isolation: expects per-component ablations, controlled experiments, and analyses that quantify each contribution; will lower score if interactions are not clearly disentangled.
Demands statistical significance testing and detailed error analysis for claimed improvements on benchmarks; will discount raw percentage gains that lack variance estimates or qualitative analysis.
Penalises reliance on large external resources or pipeline components (e.g., pretrained parsers) unless the paper clearly quantifies their contribution and robustness.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Skeptical, focuses on theoretical grounding and clarity of causal claims; grants credit only when empirical claims are accompanied by rigorous ablations, analysis, or theoretical insight.

reviewer_b.name: Empirical Systems Enthusiast
reviewer_b.background: Senior research scientist in NLP with extensive experience building end-to-end relation extraction and information extraction systems for real-world applications. Regularly evaluates models on standard benchmarks and prioritises practical impact.
traits: Rewards substantial empirical improvements on standard benchmarks (gives positive bias for reproducible SOTA gains ≥1% on established datasets like ACE05/CONLL04).

Favors well-engineered end-to-end models and practical integration of linguistic features (e.g., dependency parser encodings, segment representations); will up-score papers that convincingly improve real task performance even if theoretical analysis is limited.

Prefers comprehensive empirical methodology: multiple baselines (including strong recent neural and statistical models), ablations of major components, and use of standard splits and metrics; will penalise only minor gaps in analysis but not lack of formal proofs.

Values techniques that are likely to transfer to production or downstream tasks (e.g., beam-search global optimization, joint entity-relation modeling); awards extra credit for clear engineering and reproducibility details.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Pragmatic and results-driven: emphasizes strong experimental validation, clear comparisons to baselines, and demonstrable real-world gains; tolerates limited theory when experiments are thorough and improvements are substantial.
